title: Foundational capital hand
subtitle: Edward Johnston's broad-edge capitals as the paired partner to the Foundational minuscule
excerpt: Johnston's capital hand was designed to be written with the same broad-edge nib at the same 30-degree pen angle as the Foundational minuscule. The capitals are derived from Roman inscriptional forms but written with a dip pen at writing speed, not cut or painted. They work as standalone display lettering and as the proper companion to a Foundational page.
sourceNotes: Edward Johnston, Writing and Illuminating and Lettering (1906), chapters on capital letters and the Foundational hand. Internet Archive scans available. Johnston's capital letterforms are described and demonstrated throughout the text.

glossaryTerms.definition: The height of the lowercase letters of a calligraphic alphabet, measured from baseline to the top of a letter such as x, n, or o, not counting ascenders or descenders.
The height of the capital letters, typically 7 to 8 nib widths for the Foundational capital hand.
A stroke made at a steep angle to the baseline where the nib travels along its thin edge, producing a narrow line rather than a full-width stroke.

content.text: Johnston's capital hand was never intended to be a separate alphabet from the Foundational minuscule. It shares the same nib angle, the same letterform proportions, and the same visual weight. The capitals and the lowercase sit together on the same page. The reader should not notice any change of tool or hand.
What you need
Nib: a Mitchell Roundhand 3 for practice. The capital letters are written at 7 nib widths high (
cap-height
), which is slightly taller than the Foundational minuscule's 5 nib widths
x-height
Rule two guide lines for the capitals: baseline and cap-height. Work in sets of 5 lines at first so the hand learns the height by repetition.
Nib angle and pen hold

Pen held at 30 degrees from the baseline throughout. The same hold as for the Foundational minuscule: pen resting between the index and middle fingers, thumb supporting, hand on the side of the little finger. The 30-degree angle produces a thick diagonal on strokes going down-right and a
thin diagonal
on strokes going up-right.
Stroke orders for key capitals
O: two strokes. Stroke 1 is the left half arc, from 11 o'clock to 5 o'clock. Stroke 2 is the right half arc, from 1 o'clock to 7 o'clock, overlapping at the widest points.
C: one stroke. Derived from the O left arc, with the exit at 3 o'clock rather than completing the full oval. The top of the C ends with a slight upward flick.
H: three strokes. Stroke 1: left stem, top to bottom. Stroke 2: right stem, top to bottom. Stroke 3: crossbar, left to right, placed just above mid-height of the letter.

A: three strokes. Stroke 1: left diagonal, top-left to baseline (a thick stroke at 30-degree angle). Stroke 2: right diagonal, top-right to baseline (a thin stroke at 30-degree angle). Stroke 3: crossbar, left to right, at approximately mid-height.

M: four strokes. Stroke 1: left stem. Stroke 2: first diagonal, descending from the top of stroke 1 to the baseline midpoint. Stroke 3: second diagonal, ascending from the baseline midpoint to the top of the right stem position. Stroke 4: right stem. The V shape of M descends only to the baseline, not below it.
Common mistakes

attrs.symptom: Capitals look too narrow, like compressed versions of the Roman alphabet
attrs.cause: The letters are being written to the proportions of the minuscule rather than the wider Roman capital proportions.

content.text: The Foundational capitals follow Roman inscriptional proportions. O and C are nearly as wide as they are tall; H, N, U, Z are roughly square; only I, J, and L are narrow. Widen O first; the other letters will fall into proportion.

attrs.symptom: The crossbar of H sits too high, giving the letter a heavy bottom half
attrs.cause: The crossbar has been placed at the optical midpoint of the letter, which sits slightly above the true midpoint.

content.text: Place the H crossbar at slightly above the mathematical midpoint (about 55% of the cap-height). The optical illusion makes this feel lower than it is, which is why the crossbar feels centred.
Where it leads

Once the basic Foundational capitals are fluent, try combining them with the Foundational minuscule in a full quote or inscription. The next step is the Roman inscriptional capital, described in the Roman Capitals tutorial. That form takes the same proportions into a painted or incised tradition rather than a nib-written one.
